meta.title: F20 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 1 Series F20 engine — N13, N20, B38, B48, N47, B47, N55, B58. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 1 Series F20 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F20 engine typically costs £1,800–£8,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the N13 1.6 petrol sits at £1,800–£4,000, while the N55 M135i can reach £8,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on engine and specialist location. Evaluate the car's value carefully before committing.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series F20 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30 straight-six in the M140i is considered the most robust engine in the F20 range — BMW's B58 has a strong reliability reputation with fewer known issues than the N55 it replaced. The B38, B47 and B48 family are also significantly more reliable than the older N13 and N47 units [PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Page].

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 1 Series F20?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain stretch on the N47 diesel (pre-LCI) and N13 petrol (pre-LCI) are the most significant engine problems. The post-LCI B-family engines can suffer from cooling system leaks. The N55 M135i can have HPFP and VANOS issues. The B47 diesel's EGR cooler is a known weak point.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 1 Series F20 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and which side of the February 2015 facelift your car sits on. Post-LCI cars with B38/B47/B48/B58 engines are significantly more reliable than pre-LCI cars with N13/N47 engines. The B58 M140i is the most robust six-cylinder. The N47 diesel carries the biggest risk regardless of facelift.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series F20 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is a hard avoid, but the pre-LCI N47 diesel requires very careful consideration — timing chain stretch is well-documented and a full replacement costs £3,200–£6,000 [BMW-QUOTE]. Without documented chain work, it's a higher-risk purchase. The N13 petrol also has chain issues, though less expensive to address.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F20 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle on N47 and N13 engines is the classic early warning of timing chain wear — investigate immediately. On B-family engines, coolant loss is the key warning sign. Rough running, blue smoke, or warning lights on any engine are also key warning signs. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 1 Series F20 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000 F20, a £3,200–£6,000 N47 recon is 40–75% — borderline. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 80–150% — walk away. Our 70% BMW scrap threshold is the line. For M140i cars (£10,000–£18,000+), even the most expensive replacement is viable [BMW-VERIFIED].

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 1 Series F20 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 straight-six can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing. The B38, B47 and B48 family are also durable, with 120,000+ miles achievable. The N47 and N13 are limited by their timing chain design; addressed proactively, either can reach 120,000+ miles, but many fail earlier without intervention.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F20 and the F40 1 Series?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F20 (2011–2019) is the second-generation 1 Series and the last with rear-wheel drive — a layout that enthusiasts prize. The F40 (2019–present) is the third-generation and switched to front-wheel drive (except for top-spec xDrive models). The F40 uses BMW's new FAAR platform and offers different engine choices.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 1 Series F20 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the engine block. Tell us your variant and approximate year (pre- or post-February 2015) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 1 Series F20 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F20/F21 1 Series — spanning three engine generations (N13, N20, N47, N55, B38, B37, B47, B48, B58) across petrol, diesel and six-cylinder variants.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] pending dedicated Engine Pages. The B58 straight-six in the M140i is considered the most robust engine in the range — BMW's B58 has a strong reliability reputation. The B38, B47 and B48 family (2015-onwards) are significantly more reliable than the older N13 and N47 units they replaced. The N13 petrol (Prince engine co-developed with PSA) and N47 diesel carry known timing chain risks.

overview.intro: The F20 is the second generation of the BMW 1 Series, launched in September 2011 as the successor to the E87. It introduced a longer wheelbase (+30mm), wider track (+51mm front, +72mm rear), and improved interior space while retaining rear-wheel drive — a layout that set it apart from front-driven hatchback competitors. The engine line-up was all-turbocharged from launch, initially featuring 1.6-litre four-cylinder petrols (N13) and 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesels (N47). A significant February 2015 facelift (LCI) brought new engine families — the B38 three-cylinder, B48 four-cylinder and B58 six-cylinder — replacing the older N13, N20 and N47 units. The range-topper evolved from the N55-powered M135i to the B58-powered M140i in 2016.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L7 (rear-wheel drive) • Predecessor: E87 1 Series • Successor: F40 1 Series (front-wheel drive) • Sibling body styles in this generation: F21 (3-door hatchback).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F20 1 Series generated 344 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the diesel variants (116d, 118d, 120d) as the range reaches affordable used-car territory.

items.quote: BMW's B58 straight-six is a masterpiece — smooth, responsive, and robust. The M140i is the last of the rear-drive 1 Series hot hatches, and this engine is the highlight. Fewer known issues than the N55 it replaced, and a stronger bottom end.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who want the ultimate F20 experience.

items.quote: The B47 is a significant improvement over the N47 — timing chain issues are far less frequent, and the engine is smoother and more refined. The 120d (190 bhp) offers the best blend of performance and economy.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy without the N47's timing chain anxiety.

items.quote: The N47 diesel family carries the infamous rear-mounted timing chain risk — cold-start rattle is the warning, and a snapped chain means full replacement. The 125d's twin-turbo setup adds further complexity.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a budget who are willing to accept the chain risk.

items.quote: The N55 is a great engine, but it's the most expensive to replace in the F20 range — HPFP, VANOS and turbo issues can add up quickly. The B58 that replaced it is more robust.
items.whoItsFor: M135i buyers with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre B48 petrol is the sweet spot — more reliable than the N13 it replaced, with strong performance and reasonable running costs. The 125i (224 bhp) offers near-M135i performance without the six-cylinder running costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol performance without the M135i's premium.

items.quote: The 1.5-litre three-cylinder B38 is often dismissed as 'the entry-level engine' — but it's a gem. Smooth, characterful, and significantly more reliable than the N13 it replaced. The 118i (136 bhp) is surprisingly punchy and returns excellent fuel economy.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise running costs and don't need six-cylinder power.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — chain issues common on early cars.
Solid Choice — more robust than N13.
Safe Bet — the most reliable petrols in the range.
High Risk — rear chain is this engine's defining weakness.
Solid Choice — significantly improved over N47.
Premium Cost — fast, but costly to maintain.
Best Petrol — the most robust six-cylinder in the range.

economicsRule.title: The F20 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£6,000 N47 recon against a £3,000–£12,000 F20 sits at 27–200% of the car's value【EM-VERIFIED】. On a sub-£4,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW scrap threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 40–75% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For B58 M140i cars, the high retained value (£10,000–£18,000+) means replacement is almost always viable.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F20 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: B38 116i/118i — simple three-cylinder, fewer failure modes, cheap parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F20 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N55 M135i — HPFP and VANOS repairs add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F20 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: B47 120d (post-LCI) — strong performance, excellent economy, and none of the N47's timing chain anxiety.

problems.title: N47 Diesel Timing Chain Stretch (Pre-LCI)
problems.rootCause: The rear-mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time — cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning. The 125d's twin-turbo setup adds additional strain.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on any N47 diesel F20, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£6,000 replacement that follows if ignored. On an F20 worth under £4,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 diesel engine prices for your F20 →

problems.title: N13 Petrol Timing Chain & VANOS Issues (Pre-LCI)
problems.rootCause: The N13 (Prince engine, co-developed with PSA) can suffer from timing chain tensioner wear and VANOS solenoid failure. The chain is located at the front of the engine, making replacement less labour-intensive than the N47, but failure is still catastrophic if ignored.

tieredCosts.work: Chain, tensioner and VANOS solenoid replacement

problems.recommendation: Have the timing chain and VANOS system inspected at 60,000 miles — a £600–£1,500 preventative service is far cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. Early N13s are the highest risk.

cta.label: Compare N13 petrol engine prices for your F20 →

problems.title: B38 / B48 / B58 Cooling System Leaks (Post-LCI)
problems.rootCause: The B-family engines (B38, B48, B58) are generally reliable, but they can suffer from coolant leaks from the water pump and plastic cooling system components — a known weakness on BMW's modular engine family.

tieredCosts.work: Full engine replacement if overheating caused damage

problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system inspected annually and replace the water pump as preventative maintenance at 60,000 miles — a £400–£800 specialist job is trivial against the £3,800+ engine replacement that follows an ignored coolant leak.

cta.label: Compare B38/B48/B58 engine prices for your F20 →

problems.title: B47 Diesel EGR Cooler Failure (Post-LCI)
problems.rootCause: The B47's EGR cooler can develop internal leaks, allowing coolant to enter the exhaust system. This can lead to reduced performance and, in extreme cases, engine damage. BMW issued recalls for this issue on some models.

tieredCosts.work: Full engine replacement if coolant contamination caused damage

problems.recommendation: Check your B47 F20's VIN against any outstanding EGR cooler recalls — if affected, the repair is free. If not, have the EGR cooler inspected at 60,000 miles — a £500–£1,000 replacement is cheap insurance against a £4,000+ engine failure.

cta.label: Compare B47 diesel engine prices for your F20 →

replacementCosts.h2: F20 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the EM_Proprietary_Data baseline figures — F20 1 Series shares the L7 platform with other BMW models, with similar engine-bay access, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are F20 (5-door) and F21 (3-door) 1 Series. The 116d EfficientDynamics Edition (2012–2015) used a 1.6-litre N47 diesel rather than the 2.0-litre. M135i (2012–2016) used the N55 six-cylinder; M140i (2016–2019) used the B58 six-cylinder. Post-LCI (2015–2019) models switched from N13/N20/N47 to B38/B48/B47/B58 engines.
